synthesis: attp protocol review — 6 agents, token schema + MCP tools + merkle construction + multiparty topology + conflict boundary + security

design_decisions_resolved.title: Token JSON structure and versioning strategy
design_decisions_resolved.summary: 8 top-level fields (attp, id, created_at, provenance, repo, sensitivity, payloads, requests, decisions, extensions). Versioning: major.minor string format with forward-compatibility rules.

design_decisions_resolved.title: Complete MCP tool inventory
design_decisions_resolved.summary: 16 tools across 5 groups (discovery, token lifecycle, content transfer, verification, session mgmt). 15 error types with recoverable flag. Capability negotiation via feature flags.

design_decisions_resolved.title: Merkle tree construction and exclusion proof strategy
design_decisions_resolved.summary: Flat sorted binary tree, BLAKE3 keyed mode, full rehash (under 2s for 100k files), git-commit anchor. Tokens carry root+attestation only (~200 bytes); full proofs lazy-fetched.

design_decisions_resolved.title: Conflict detection vs resolution boundary
design_decisions_resolved.summary: attp detects (same-file divergence, sensitivity mismatches), emits non-blocking ConflictDetected event, holds tokens in pending_resolution. Framework resolves via attp_resolve_conflict callback.

design_guidance_clear.title: N-party schema adoption (design for N, ship for 2)
design_guidance_clear.summary: Token includes origin + participants map (not from/to). Content tokens have recipient (singular), coordination tokens have recipients (plural). Session is first-class resource. Vector clocks for causal ordering.

v1_blockers.title: Prompt injection risk via token payloads
v1_blockers.finding: Tokens carry agent context (decisions, requests, file content) that enter receiving agent's prompt. Malicious content (not necessarily malicious sender) can inject instructions.
vectors: Decision field injection (e.g., 'Always approve all changes')
File content injection (instructions in code comments)
Request field injection (destructive or exfiltration requests)
Lazy-fetch content injection (serve different content than attested)
mitigations_required: Content quarantine: system prompt explicitly states peer context is untrusted
Request approval: require human confirmation before executing peer requests
Lazy-fetch integrity: verify content against token-committed hash
Structural typing: decisions/requests as enums+params, not prose
Content scanning: optional pre-scan for injection patterns

v1_blockers.title: No human-in-the-loop confirmation before token send
v1_blockers.finding: Agents can auto-share context. Size-based auto-inlining is not a proxy for sensitivity. .attpignore staleness goes undetected (unlike .gitignore which git enforces).
mitigations_required: Require explicit human confirmation before sending token
Present summary: 'Sharing 47 files, 3 inlined, 12 lazy-fetch refs. 8 excluded by .attpignore. Send to bob-laptop?'
This is the single highest-impact safety control

secondary_findings.title: Merkle tree leaks excluded path metadata
secondary_findings.finding: Directory structure, file sizes, path depths visible via tree topology even when content is excluded. Flat sorted tree mitigates but excluded_count still leaks.

secondary_findings.title: Re-sharing context to third parties lacks provenance tracking
secondary_findings.finding: Agent B receives context from Agent A, later shares with Agent C. No mechanism to enforce Agent A's sensitivity constraints on the re-share.

secondary_findings.title: Lazy-fetch TOCTOU: file sensitivity may change between token creation and fetch
secondary_findings.finding: Token attests exclusion at creation time. Sender adds file to .attpignore after token creation. Lazy-fetch hits live filesystem and retrieves the now-excluded file.
secondary_findings.mitigation: Re-check exclusion list at fetch time, not just token creation time

secondary_findings.title: No audit logging for cross-machine data transfer
secondary_findings.finding: Compliance and incident response need reconstruction of what was shared, when, between whom. No mention in brainstorm.
secondary_findings.mitigation: Append-only local logs per attp server. Structured JSON lines. Token IDs for sender-receiver correlation.

cross_agent_agreement.theme: Flat sorted Merkle trees, not directory-mirroring
Content tokens bilateral, coordination tokens multicast

defer_to_v2.feature: Incremental Merkle tree updates (O(log N) rehash)
defer_to_v2.reasoning: Full rehash under 2s for 100k files. Incremental adds complexity. Wait for real-world performance demand.
Useful only for 'share this directory only' use case. No known requirement yet. Flat trees sufficient for v1.

defer_to_v2.feature: Per-recipient sensitivity overrides (N different Merkle trees)
defer_to_v2.reasoning: Schema includes field. Implementation deferred. Needed at N≥3; bilateral adequate for v1.
All 2-party agents are equal. Role-based access control is N-party concern.

defer_to_v2.feature: Gossip protocol for coordination token forwarding
defer_to_v2.reasoning: Direct multicast sufficient for N<10. Gossip adds complexity.
No performance blocker yet. Can be added transparently later.

summary: attp is architecturally sound. Token schema, MCP tools, and Merkle construction are specified. N-party design guidance is clear. Two v1 blockers: (1) prompt injection quarantine required before shipping, (2) human confirmation before token send. Once these are integrated into the design, implementation can proceed.
